critique.summary: The article appears to be a webpage listing various digital collections and resources available through the Internet Archive, including audio, images, software, texts, videos, and mobile apps. It provides categories and featured items but does not present a narrative or argument.
mainPoints: The Internet Archive offers a wide range of digital media including audio recordings, images, software, texts, and videos.
Collections include live music, old time radio, NASA images, vintage software, and historical texts.
There are specialized sections for mobile apps and browser extensions related to the Wayback Machine.
The page includes links for donations, volunteering, and contact information for the Internet Archive.
critique.truthfulness: The content is factual in describing the types of digital collections and resources available through the Internet Archive. The Internet Archive is a well-known nonprofit digital library that provides free access to cultural artifacts in digital form. The categories and items listed correspond to known collections hosted by the Internet Archive, making the information accurate and truthful.
considerations: The article does not provide detailed descriptions or context for the collections, which may limit understanding for new users.
No sources or external references are cited, but given the nature of the content as a resource listing, this is typical and acceptable.
The page does not discuss privacy, copyright, or access restrictions that may apply to some materials.
There is no analysis or critique of the quality or comprehensiveness of the collections.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around Kagi's Small Web product, with users expressing mixed feelings about its definition and functionality. Some appreciate the curated, niche content approach while others find it limited or frustrating compared to broader or older search experiences. There are also suggestions for improvements and comparisons to other platforms.

comments.author: hamdingers
comments.text:
I'm a Kagi search/assistant user and advocate but the "small web" product is a frustrating misnomer.

To me the small web is any little website that was created to be interesting rather than to sell me something. That includes stuff like neocities, "shrine" type sites, single purpose sites, fandom portals, web experiments, etc.

Unfortunately Kagi's definition of "small web" is: blog or webcomic. You must have an RSS feed and it must have recent posts. That rules out so much interesting stuff I don't understand the point.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately highlights the narrow criteria Kagi uses for its Small Web, which limits the diversity of sites included.

comments.author: freetonik
comments.text:
On a similar note, I maintain and grow a manually curated collection of personal blogs with valid RSS feeds: https://minifeed.net/blogs

The criteria is simple: human-written (as much as I can validate myself), in English (for now), with valid RSS feed, and not a micro-blog (so, more than just feed of links or short tweet-like messages).

Similar to Kagi's Small Web viewer, or StumbleUpon-style viewer: you can get a random listing of blogs [1] or a random listing of posts from all blogs [2]. Feeds and posts are indexed, so full-text search works across all blogs. When possible and permitted by robots.txt, text is scraped for searching, so even if some text is omitted in the RSS feed by the author, search should work.

Though I do plan to implement a similar "view one random post at source" kind of view, soon.

UPD: Feel free to submit a blog, including your own! [3]

[1] https://minifeed.net/blogs/by/random

[2] https://minifeed.net/global/random

[3] https://minifeed.net/suggest
comments.analysis: Provides a relevant alternative and detailed explanation of similar curation efforts, supporting the discussion with concrete examples.

comments.author: modernerd
comments.text:
Great idea. Index is based on submitted sites:

https://github.com/kagisearch/smallweb/blob/main/smallweb.tx...

There is also Small Comic:

https://kagi.com/smallweb/?comic

https://github.com/kagisearch/smallweb/blob/main/smallcomic....

And Small YouTube:

https://kagi.com/smallweb/?yt

https://github.com/kagisearch/smallweb/blob/main/smallyt.txt
comments.analysis: Offers factual information about the source and structure of Kagi's Small Web, adding clarity to how the product is curated.

comments.author: ArtificeAccount
comments.text:
I've been using the Kagi search engine for months now and I'm not impressed. I bought into it because there were a lot of posts saying that it was "just like old Google" but this has not been my experience. It's the same as new Google, you can type in what you're looking for exactly and you'll get random sort-of related websites.

I remember when you could half-remember a comment from a website, type that into Google, and get taken to the article you were looking for. That was back in like 2010. To me that's the old, and useful, search engine that I want.
comments.analysis: Expresses a subjective but valid critique based on personal experience, noting a perceived decline in search precision.

comments.author: yashasolutions
comments.text:
StumbleUpon is that you?

Jokes aside, it's really nice and I can totally see becoming addictive. Kudos to Kagi team for an other user oriented product. (as a side note, I am using Kagi daily and i didn't know about this tool)
comments.analysis: Positive but brief comment appreciating the product, though lacking detailed critique or evidence.

comments.author: marcd35
comments.text:
I'm one of the contributors for the Obsidian RSS reader (https://github.com/amatya-aditya/obsidian-rss-dashboard) and one of my first contributions to the project was adding in the Kagi RSS feed to the discover page!

It refreshes every 5 hours and shows you the most recent blogs published on Kagi. Check it out!
comments.analysis: Provides factual information about integration with another tool, showing practical use of Kagi's RSS feed.

comments.author: azangru
comments.text: The first random page it returned to me was this — https://gaultier.github.io/blog/how_to_make_your_own_static_... — which was about building one's own static site generator, which I really liked. I did not realise when I closed that page how hard it would be to find it again, because, of course every new visit to Kagi returns a different page :-)
comments.analysis: Highlights a usability issue with the random page feature, a valid user experience concern.

comments.author: erremerre
comments.text:
I like the idea, but would like to be able to select a language and see the small web of that language. There are more languages than English, and this tool could make them thrive.

Also somehow if they are clever, they could use this for those translation system they are using, but please let us select our own language without feeding automatic translation like youtube does).
comments.analysis: Makes a reasonable suggestion for improvement to support multilingual content, enhancing inclusivity.

comments.author: Venn1
comments.text:
Ah, this might explain the traffic from Kagi a week or so ago. I've been scratching my head over that one. I just checked, and my wee little blog is listed in smallweb.txt. Neat!

Curious what goes on behind the Next Post and Show Similar buttons.
comments.analysis: Shares personal experience confirming inclusion and expresses curiosity about features, contributing to discussion.

comments.author: arscan
comments.text:
I do love the concept, but a little part of me died
each time I came across an article with a very strong AI voice. That just feels antithetical to the ‘small web’ ethos because it obscures the ‘neighbor’ behind it.
comments.analysis: Raises a valid cultural concern about AI-generated content diluting the personal feel of small web sites.

comments.author: Aachen
comments.text:
Does it work for you guys to go to about and then click on the "list" link?

For me it says I'm blocked due to hitting a "secondary" rate limit (don't understand what that means). I don't think I've opened a page on github yet today so clearly it's a lie. Is it the referer that triggers this?

In general, freeloading the "small web" on a Microsoft service is kind of ironic. Being blocked by algorithms that try to detect if you're really human is precisely one of the things one would hope to get away from by using small, personal websites
comments.analysis: Points out a technical issue and irony related to hosting and access, relevant but less central to product critique.

comments.author: HelloUsername
comments.text:
Related recent blog post "Small Web Just Got Bigger" https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=47366230 13-march-2026

Previous post 7-sept-2023 https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=37420281 185 comments. And https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=39476015 23-feb-2023 36 comments
comments.analysis: Provides useful references for further reading but adds little original analysis.

comments.author: input_sh
comments.text: Could've at least checked if the website even allows embedding before embedding it, I found two by randomly clicking around that don't.
comments.analysis: Valid technical critique about embedding practices that could improve user experience.

comments.author: rusakov-field
comments.text: My blog was getting traffic from that domain! So that is what that is.
comments.analysis: Simple confirmation of referral traffic source, factual but limited in scope.

comments.author: emehex
comments.text: StumbleUpon?
comments.analysis: A brief, unclear comment that adds little to the discussion.

comments.author: the_axiom
comments.text: I have the impression that Kagi is trending here every month
comments.analysis: A vague observation about Kagi's popularity without substantive content.

comments.author: papakatsu
comments.text: I thought this was going to be about payment processing for shareware applications.
comments.analysis: Off-topic comment that does not contribute to the discussion.

comments.author: titzer
comments.text:
How do we keep getting surprised by enshittification!?

The worst case scenario is that AI runs everything, we have no skills, and are completely dependent on it...and it shows us crummy commercials and subtly steers us to paid placement with no recourse whatsoever. I hate this possible future, but this is where the money will lead.
comments.analysis: Raises a broader societal concern about AI and commercialization, relevant but somewhat speculative.

comments.author: unbindableisaac
comments.text:
Bit bummed. The first random page I landed on was a really interesting article for me. The custom cursor (well why not) had me struggling to following a link, and instinctively I refreshed the page. I ended up somewhere else in the haystack with ostensibly no way back to that particular article.

Perhaps I'm yelling into the void here, but what would be great is when first landing at kagi.com/smallweb, the url query parameter would be somehow set, as it is when "Next Post" is clicked.
comments.analysis: Valid user experience complaint about navigation and usability on the Small Web site.

comments.author: lelanthran
comments.text:
This is fantastic - how long do I have to press F5 before my blog shows up?

:-)
comments.analysis: Lighthearted comment with little analytical value.

comments.author: drstewart
comments.text: Some context would be helpful
comments.analysis: A generic request for more information without adding insight.

comments.author: lich_king
comments.text:
I think it shows the limits of hand curation. It's a tiny, human-reviewed slice of the "small web", only allowing a subset of blogs... but if you select the "programming" category and click around for a short while, you get a fair amount of obvious AI slop.

I don't think it's Kagi's fault, but I guess it's depressing in a way. A lot of "small web" bloggers dream of being a part of the "big web", and when they get a cheat button, they have no second thoughts about mashing it.
comments.analysis: Insightful critique about limitations of curation and presence of AI content diluting quality.

comments.author: 7777777phil
comments.text: I run a Hugo blog and I get more interesting referral traffic from Kagi's small web index than from Google at this point. 5,000 curated sites is small enough to be useful most "indie web" directories are graveyards unfortunately..
comments.analysis: Provides a positive personal experience supporting Kagi's value for niche traffic.

comments.author: jwelten
comments.text: Interesting, really like the idea. Maybe in the future a possibility to use it in multiple languages
comments.analysis: Offers a constructive suggestion for improvement, consistent with other multilingual comments.

comments.author: sam_goody
comments.text:
So, basically, a random site from their index of ~30,000 sites.

You can choose similar sites by index.

But what are the criterion to have your site listed here, or how it will prevent this from just becoming a massive gamified advertising index, or anything more about "why these?" is not obvious to me.

Can anyone explain what is special about these sites specifically, or where this project is going?
comments.analysis: Raises important questions about curation transparency and potential for abuse.

comments.author: myth_drannon
comments.text: Too many AI or relegion related sites.
comments.analysis: Expresses a subjective observation about content imbalance, plausible but anecdotal.

comments.author: apples_oranges
comments.text: A bit off topic, but I noticed I hardly ever use search anymore. It's just google.com/ai in 99% of cases. I believe in the future, search engines must go in this direction ..
comments.analysis: Insightful comment about changing search habits and future trends in search technology.

comments.author: myth_drannon
comments.text:
Can we just agree that the internet is broken and no amount of boutique search solutions will save it? Kagi, DDG, Google they are all trying to do a search in a pile of steaming sh*, in a hope of finding that shining diamond.

Quite possible that people will come up with a solution eventually. Like Samizdat was a solution to censorship and a broken publishing system in USSR.
comments.analysis: Philosophical and somewhat pessimistic view on internet search, relevant but speculative.

comments.author: WhereIsTheTruth
comments.text: Kagi wants to exist in a world that doesn't need it anymore
comments.analysis: A critical, somewhat fatalistic opinion about Kagi's relevance, subjective and less substantiated.
